author: @MikeGrayM70
text: My 1980 Honda Civic has a mechanical regulator like that! It actually controls the Voltage output by regulating power to the alternator's rotor. So as it spins up with rpms, the rotor electromagnet gets weaker and the engine has an easier time rotating it. It's been working perfectly for decades. :)

text:
The new way to regulate the voltage on an alternator is by the computer controlling the excitation pulse. You could have a good alternator and a bad computer and you'd be scratching your head trying to figure out why your battery isn't charging. Oh modern technology. Ya gotta love it. Lol
Good video as always. Thank you Dr. Smith.

author: @coldfinger459sub0
text: That mechanical regulator made me remember when I was a child with my father setting up regulators for old generators on cars or mechanical. Adjusting mechanical regulators for their cut in and cut out voltage.

replies.author: @JamesWilson-pq9qp
replies.text: Yea It seems like yesterday ,1963 when all the cars changed over to alternators. Those old regulators would chatter away keeping the voltage at 13.8 (or was it 14.7?).  Ah remember seeing the sparks coming off those brushes on the generators at night.  The maintenance we use to have to do to keep a car running.

replies.author: @coldfinger459sub0
replies.text: @JamesWilson-pq9qp And they say they don’t build things like they used to . Thank God they don’t build things like they used to. Packing wheelbearings on a car every 15,000 miles or so. Adjusting or changing points in a distributor cap and then you would have to set a time in after that. All those zerk fitting that needed to be greased on the suspension. Replacing spark plug wires. Changing inner tubes in a tire after a flat. Flushing and changing coolant every year. I don’t miss any of that.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest mate - I used to love that stuff

replies.author: @JamesWilson-pq9qp
replies.text: @coldfinger459sub0 So true! It's funny how we forget the technological advances we have seen. We use to burn added lead to gasoline to lubricated the valves - how stupid we were.

replies.author: @JamesWilson-pq9qp

text: Is that luke in the background yelling?  :P  Anyway, yeah,  i recall my dad built a 110V(ish) lead acid SLA charger,   it was dead simple,  using a 4way bridge and a chunky resistor. he fed it to 8 used recycled 12V gell cell batteries,  then across  the mains, he had a lightbulb and a capacitor. once the capacitor rose above 110V it it would light the bulb and bleed off some power making the batteries at more of a trickle charge.  actually the light bulb may have been the resistor.  ... anyway,  it was kinda neat,   lots of brushed ac motors worked fine with it.. author: @kreynolds1123
text: One can improve the efficiency by using a mechanical switch in a buck converter to convert high voltage low current to low voltage high current. Then voltage regulation doesn't have as much voltage to drop.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
With vehicle alternators, the field coils output is regulated by changing the current in the armature to change the strength of it's magnetic field.  If  the voltage sense at the battery senses a drop in voltage , the voltage regulator sends more current to the armature to generate a stronger magnet field on the armature that in turn generates a bit more voltage on the field coils. If the battery voltage sense wire  detects the battery terminal is at full charge, the voltage regulators send less current to the armature  thus generate less off a magnetic field thus lowers the output of the field coils.

On the other hand some motor bikes use a small dynamo instead of an alternator  where the voltage off the coils can get rather high and is regulated with linear voltage regulation.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @JavierChiappa
text:
I just blew one of those chinese Lead-Acid Battery chargers by connecting it backwards (I know, I know), i just opened it up thinking i may have to just change a diode or something like that. To my horror, i found it full of surface mounted components, a LM358 chip, a switch mode power supply and many other things. I just bought another one, impossible to repair that stuff without the diagram. 

I think there would be a market for this basic blocks of every offgrid installation (Solar charge controller, Inverter, Battery Charger, etc) if they are made in a very simple, sturdy way, with repairability in mind, like an included diagram, spare parts (fuses, diodes, power transistors) and many test points with their respective correct values. I may be more expensive but you buy it just once and keep repairing it!. I hate chinesium electronics so much.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I absolutely hate stuff you can't repair - it is supposed to be more efficient - personally I believe it is just cheaper to make and if - and when - it breaks all you can do is buy another - saying it is more efficient is just to get you to buy one in the first place!

replies.author: @Buzzhumma
replies.text: Most chargers and controllers these days have mosfets that that are not matches in their resistance so over time one works harder than the others and inevitably burns out . High quality commercial stuff is matched and very reliable . Very important to match the esr ! It is actually the main fault point by design ! 😣

replies.author: @JavierChiappa
replies.text: @Buzzhumma Good tip there, i will check for that problem next time some power source blows up inexplicably, lol.

author: @mouldykev
text:
Blimey, this takes me back a bit Rob... what you have just shown was in fact a replication of the very first ever car alarm system....once set off it went eeh awe ..eeh awe  from the cars horn but the actual implementation was exactly as you have just shown ( not certain how old you are but i am 63 next month so remember this well ) just as i remember a guy  i once knew that worked for Girling and he showed me this mechanical device with two belt drives from a pair of Drive shafts on a car and he asked me ..." do you know what this is Kev " i said no idea... and he said " its this new Braking system that we are working on called ABS ! " i guess i was very lucky in my early years because i knew many such a man that showed me many such things and i could go on all night... USA sent over smallest drill bit ever...we sent it back centre bored LoL Yes i knew someone that worked on that too...oh the good old days...anyhow move on a few years with charge controll and i thought i knew it all, i was on the verge of doing a deal with Motorola with my products ( which did get rubber stamped ) but this guy  i met ( from the USA ) said Nah youre doing it all wrong, the latest in charging is Delta V.... i was gobsmacked .................but we all learn from others and i still learn even now something new every day.....thanks for sending me back Robert...
regards Kev

author: @sofa-lofa4241
text:
Great tutorial on switches and timing, and nice comparison between electro-mechanical and digital, all computers are basically lots of these crammed together in a grid of Billions, Nano-Metres apart, all switching at Giga-Hertz frequencies... Same stuff... Different level... but the same level that drove both Babbage/Lovelace and modern chip designers/programmers, 

I've always thought that the nicest thing about a mechanical switch is that you can attach a pencil, run a sheet of paper past at a known rate and make the cheapest oscilloscope known to man  ¬_¬__¬___¬_¬ ~~~~~~💷🚫🤣

This solenoid/spring setup would give a sine wave, maybe a quick lesson in how PWM works and how to chop it up like a wizard 🧙‍♂️ with basic components?
I'm thinking that a detailed look at MPPT, etc. might be for the future, but as we're looking at basic switching then a PWM tutorial would be handy.

author: @benthere8051
text:
What you just described is now known as pulse-width modulation. Mechanical switches have been replaced with MOS transistors and the switching speeds are in the megahertz. Inductors store the energy and deliver it to capacitors which smooth the switched power.
I thank you for your explanation of the battery chemistry and its implementation in the past two videos.
That information is priceless and very hard to come by.
Teach us the chemistry in YouTube videos and I guarantee you an enormous audience.
Show us a way to store solar or wind power in 5-gallon buckets or other readily available containers and the chemistry involved in that process and we will beat a path to your door.
I realize we are asking for the secret of fire so I will understand if you decline.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes I know and I already have shown the 'secret of fire 'mate - it's in the videos - actually what happens is folks argue with you and each other - there is no 'beating the path to the door' well in my experience anyway. Actually people would beat a path to the door if I sang a song about falling in love, had a baby bite my finger or  if I fell off a skate board - you only need to check viral videos to see what people actually 'beat a path' for

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Great story. I did not know about these mechanical charge regulators. You are very right, the switching regulators these days does the same thing be it at much higher frequencies. The solenoid you are using in your mechanical device is still present in any step up or step down electronic regulator these day's. It can be smaller due to higher frequency. The switch you are using is still used. It will be some kind of transistor these days, probably an enhancement mode mosfet but basic principles are the same.

The main point in having these devices is efficiency of course. The same thing could be done using a dissipating regulator but it would be hot in use and the energy loss would be huge.

Thanks for the video.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thank you mate and thank you for taking the time to post

author: @AtlasReburdened
text: I didn't think that the output of an alternator is passed through a regulator on the way to the battery. I though the regulation of the output was accomplished by sensing the output and limiting the current through the field coil when that output rose above a certain voltage, so that the action of the rotor actually produced less due to having a deminished field to 'act upon'. Thus eliminating the need for a regulator that can handle the double digit amps that go to the battery, and instead substituting a regulator that only needs to handle the low single digit amps going through the field coil. Also Eliminating the enormous waste energy that would result from directly regulating the output.

replies.author: @JavierChiappa
replies.text: I think you are right, the regulator on the car alternator just powers off the field in the rotor part when the battery is full. It also stops consuming "torque" from the motor when you turn off the fields. It's such an ingenious an efficient thing, it's a shame it has to turn very fast to be effective, otherwise we could just use it in wind generators directly and it would work great.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's just a way of conceptualising mate - after all if I tell you the atom is a nucleus surrounded by electrons in orbits you will basically agree even though you know it's wrong. Sometimes a simpler concept helps grab the main principle and the idea here is just the logic of - 'it generates too much - turn it off' - it doesn't, at this stage, matter much how that is accomplished

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: stopping generating when the capacity to generate is still there because the system is 'full' is throwing away power mate - it just depends on how you view it

author: @gusmartin6053
text: So you could make a crude voltage regulator out of a relay. Fascinating!

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: Buck converter needs a switch. A relay can serve as that switch. As a mechanical switch, its slow, and waste more energy to switch than say a mosfet, and it will eventually fail. Furthermore induction spikes will erode the switch contacts faster so you'll want to engineer for that too.

replies.author: @gusmartin6053
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 Good point. The same concept is used in buck converters, with countless benefits. I guess its just an interesting thought that you could use a relay as a regulator. It is of course very impractical and not a method to seriously consider.🍻

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes you could mate - there are limitations - of course - but it is not in the least impractical. It is actually the way it was done - you just have to work with or accept the limits. Actually, I believe very similar devices are still used in high power and high voltage applications as solid state can't handle the voltages and currents involved
